coredata.dc:title: Novel lithium production process using desalination wastewater and waste heat from natural gas combined cycle

coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Hyejeong
Lim, Jonghun
Kim, Junghwan
coredata.dc:description: The rapid growth of lithium-ion battery applications has significantly increased the demand for lithium. Continental brines store approximately 60% of lithium resources, and are commonly used for lithium extraction. However, this process is time-consuming and requires large amounts of Na2CO3 and heat, thus having a low production rate and low economic feasibility. This study presents a novel lithium production process that reduces production time and overcomes high-energy consumption by leveraging waste heat from a natural gas combined cycle (NGCC) and using desalination wastewater as a source. The process model based on validated experimental data consists of four stages: (1) liquid natural gas (LNG) regasification, (2) NGCC power generation, (3) desalination, and (4) recovery of lithium from desalination wastewater. Economic analysis was performed to assess the payback period and demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed method. The results indicate that 108.5 tons/year of Li2CO3 can be produced using the concentrated wastewater generated by the hybrid desalination process. Furthermore, NaOH absorbents can be synthesized using desalination wastewater, capturing approximately 93.0% of CO2 and achieving 48.7% energy savings through waste heat recovery. The proposed process is economically feasible, with a net present value of 2,827 million USD and a payback period of 0.31 years. Therefore, this study provides valuable insight into the efficient and environmentally friendly production of lithium using recovered waste heat and desalination wastewater.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid growth of lithium-ion battery applications has significantly increased the demand for lithium. Continental brines store approximately 60% of lithium resources, and are commonly used for lithium extraction. However, this process is time-consuming and requires large amounts of Na2CO3 and heat, thus having a low production rate and low economic feasibility. This study presents a novel lithium production process that reduces production time and overcomes high-energy consumption by leveraging waste heat from a natural gas combined cycle (NGCC) and using desalination wastewater as a source. The process model based on validated experimental data consists of four stages: (1) liquid natural gas (LNG) regasification, (2) NGCC power generation, (3) desalination, and (4) recovery of lithium from desalination wastewater. Economic analysis was performed to assess the payback period and demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed method. The results indicate that 108.5 tons/year of Li2CO3 can be produced using the concentrated wastewater generated by the hybrid desalination process. Furthermore, NaOH absorbents can be synthesized using desalination wastewater, capturing approximately 93.0% of CO2 and achieving 48.7% energy savings through waste heat recovery. The proposed process is economically feasible, with a net present value of 2,827 million USD and a payback period of 0.31 years. Therefore, this study provides valuable insight into the efficient and environmentally friendly production of lithium using recovered waste heat and desalination wastewater.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Life span of the investment, y

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the demand for lithium has increased substantially owing to the expanding application of lithium-ion batteries in energy storage, electric vehicles, and portable devices [1,2]. As the lightest metal and the least dense solid element, lithium is an essential component of batteries [3]. Approximately 60% of lithium resources are found in the form of continental brines, making them a significant source of lithium production [4,5]. However, brines also contain other metal ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+ at considerably higher concentrations than lithium [6]. Therefore, the separation of lithium from other metal ions is critical for the extraction of high-purity lithium.

Typically, the extraction of lithium from continental brine involves concentrating the brine via solar evaporation [7,8]. Subsequently, other metal ions, such as Mg2+ and Ca2+, are separated from the concentrated brine through solvent extraction and individual metal ion precipitation. Finally, lithium is extracted in the form of Li2CO3 using Na2CO3. High-purity Li2CO3 can be obtained within a temperature range of 353–363 K, as its solubility is lowest within this range [9,10].

The conventional lithium production process faces several challenges, which can be outlined as follows. First, solar evaporation is a time-consuming method that results in low lithium production rates. During this process, brine is naturally evaporated in evaporation ponds over a period of 4–6 months (up to 24 months) [8,11,12]. Moreover, rainfall reduces evaporation efficiency, thus the process is highly susceptible to the effects of climate change. Second, the continuous supply of Na2CO3, required for the separation of Ca2+, Mg2+, and Li+ in the form of carbonates, increases the raw material costs and reduces the economic feasibility. Na2CO3 is commonly produced through the Solvay process, which involves the calcination of CaCO3 and emits a large amount of air pollutants, exacerbating environmental contamination [13,14]. Finally, to achieve high purity Li2CO3, the operating temperature during the Li2CO3 carbonation process must be maintained at a high-temperature range of 353–363 K, which increases operating costs [10,15].

Many studies have addressed the above mentioned challenges of conventional lithium production by increasing the lithium production rate using membranes, absorbents, and liquid-membrane-based electrolysis. Park et al. [16] proposed a novel membrane-based lithium recovery process, which can result in decreased process time, thus reducing capital and operating costs. Kim et al. [17] developed an electrochemical system based on a battery electrode material that can be used for efficient lithium recovery from continental brine. The proposed approach can capture Li+ approximately three times faster than the conventional processes. Liu et al. [18] proposed a novel liquid membrane electrodialysis method to selectively recover lithium from brines. Their method showed great potential in application to lithium production owing to its high selectivity, efficient energy utilization, and environmental safety. Zhou et al. [19] studied the effects of the NaPF6 concentration, phase ratio, initial pH, temperature, and MgCl2 concentration on lithium extraction. They found that under optimal conditions, the extraction efficiency increased to 78.9%.

Despite the numerous contributions of previous studies, several challenges remain unaddressed. For instance, membrane-based lithium production is marked by high energy consumption and short membrane lifespan. Furthermore, absorbent-based production processes employ acidic components, which not only reduces the lifespan of the absorbent but also increases environmental water contamination. Finally, liquid-membrane-based electrolysis can lead to solvent leakage and membrane swelling, affecting the separation efficiency during a long-term operation. In addition, the high toxicity of the ionic liquid can negatively impact the environment.

To address these challenges, this study presents a lithium production process that utilizes highly concentrated desalination wastewater to increase environmental benefits and reduce the lithium production process time. Furthermore, to decrease energy consumption, the use of recovered waste heat generated by the natural gas combined cycle (NGCC) process is proposed. The study aims to design a process that can help increase lithium production without the added environmental damage by utilizing concentrated desalination wastewater as a resource. Additionally, by using Na+ in desalination wastewater as a solvent to capture CO2 from the NGCC process and using the recovered Na2CO3 as an absorption solvent, the environmental issues associated with the desalination waste and CO2 emissions from the flue gas can be simultaneously addressed, while reducing the cost of raw materials for lithium production and achieving economic benefits. Added environmental benefits can be achieved by utilizing the Na2CO3 produced by capturing air pollutants without the need for a Solvay process that generates air pollutants. Finally, operating costs can be reduced by recovering waste heat from the NGCC process to reduce the energy required to produce high-purity Li2CO3 at high operating temperatures.

The process proposed in this study integrates LNG regasification, the NGCC, desalination, and lithium recovery from desalination wastewater. In this Section, each process and the main methodology are discussed. In Section 2.1, the proposed lithium production process using NGCC waste heat and seawater desalination wastewater is divided into parts and the flow of the process is explained. Section 2.2 shows the parameter values for process simulation. Section 2.3 presents the economic evaluation formula used in this study and its explanations.

A schematic of the proposed lithium production process using NGCC waste heat and desalination wastewater is presented in Fig. 1 . The process proposed in this study is divided into four parts: (1) LNG regasification, (2) NGCC, (3) desalination, and (4) lithium recovery from desalination wastewater.

LNG was loaded into specially insulated tanks and transported via ship to the acquisition terminal at a temperature of approximately 111 K and atmospheric pressure [20]. However, during transportation, a temperature difference of approximately 450 K occurred between the inside and outside of the tank, causing the temperature inside the tank to increase [21]. Consequently, approximately 0.15% of the total LNG transport volume regasified and generated a boil-off gas (BOG) [22]. The BOG caused an increase in the cargo tank pressure, which could lead to an explosion and result in energy loss owing to vaporization. To solve this problem, BOG was compressed, condensed, and used as feedstock. Seawater was then used as a heat source to regasify the LNG using a vaporizer [23]. Seawater containing large amounts of LNG cold energy was discarded during the LNG regasification [24]. However, LNG cold energy can be recovered in Section 2.4 for the K+ separation process and flue gas cooling reaction, which can help to reduce the required energy usage.

The NGCC process used natural gas as a fuel to generate electricity. The system comprised three main components: a gas turbine, a steam turbine, and a heat recovery steam generator (HRSG) [25]. First, natural gas underwent an LNG regasification reaction and was then mixed with air in a combustor reactor, to cause a high-temperature exothermic reaction that generated a high-temperature and high-pressure flue gas. Second, high-energy flue gas was passed through a gas turbine to generate electricity. After the gas turbine cycle, the flue gas still contained high energy; therefore, the flue gas was sent into an HRSG to recover additional energy [26]. High-temperature steam and flue gas were generated while passing through the HRSG; the generated flue gas contained a large amount of CO2 [27]. Therefore, the flue gas was cooled using LNG cold energy and processed through a CO2 capture technology. Third, the high-temperature steam produced by the HRSG passed through high- and low-pressure steam turbines to produce electricity [28]. The steam turbine outlet pressure was kept low, and a steam condenser was used to supply feed water to the boiler. The condenser used seawater as a heat source to create a condensate, whereas seawater with a large amount of thermal energy was discarded [26]. Therefore, in this process, the waste heat energy of the heated seawater was recovered and used for the seawater desalination process described in Section 2.1.3 and the lithium carbonate production process described in Section 2.1.4.

Seawater desalination used a hybrid forward osmosis (FO)-reverse osmosis (RO) process [29]. The FO process is a method that uses a semi-permeable membrane and a draw solution. The use of a draw solution is based on the osmotic gradient created between the feed and draw solutions [30]. When seawater from the feed solution and NaCl from the draw solution are injected into the FO, the feed solution with a lower concentration than that of the draw solution passes through the semipermeable membrane, and freshwater is recovered [31]. The FO process changes the flux based on the temperature of the draw solution and operating temperature. In our experiment, a draw solution heated to 318 K increased the water flux and allowed large amounts of freshwater recovery [32]. Thus, freshwater recovered via the FO process was transferred to the RO to retrieve freshwater, and the remaining concentrated seawater was discharged. The pressure of the draw solution, which has collected freshwater through the FO process, was raised using a pump before being injected into the RO process.

The RO process separated freshwater from the diluted draw solution recovered through the FO process [33]. In RO, the high-pressure solution overcomes the osmotic pressure through a permeable membrane and separates freshwater from the draw solution. Overall, RO membranes reject over 99% of single ions [33]. The separated draw solution is recovered and reused in the FO process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lithium recovery process from desalination wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, seawater was desalinated to produce freshwater and the resulting wastewater was electrolyzed to produce NaOH. The resulting NaOH solution was used to adjust the pH of the wastewater and separate metal cations, including lithium [34]. In addition, CO2 was captured from the flue gas generated during the NGCC process, and carbonation processes were developed for Mg2+, Ca2+, and Li+ to produce lithium compounds.

Desalination wastewater contains significant amounts of metal cations, such as Li+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, and Ca2+ [35]. To separate the highly soluble K+ ions, the HClO4 solution was injected to precipitate KClO4 [36]. The required low temperature of 266 K was achieved using LNG cold energy that would have otherwise been wasted during the LNG regasification process, resulting in reduced energy consumption. As high concentrations of NaOH were produced via electrolysis, that NaOH was used as a pH-adjusting solution to separate Mg2+ and Ca2+ [37]. For instance, to remove the large amount of Mg2+ from the desalination wastewater, pH was adjusted to 7–11 using an electrolysis-generated NaOH solution to precipitate and separate Mg(OH)2. Similarly, Ca2+ was precipitated with NaOH as Ca(OH)2 at pH 11–13 [38]. Furthermore, the NaOH produced by electrolysis was used as a CO2 absorbent to capture the flue gas CO2 generated in the NGCC process. After capturing the CO2 in the absorption tower, Na2CO3 containing ionic CO2 was discharged to produce MgCO3 and CaCO3 through a carbonation reaction with the separated Mg(OH)2 and Ca(OH)2 [39]. Ca(OH)2 is soluble in water and dissociates into OH– and Ca2+ when it reacts with Na2CO3 to produce CaCO3 [40] . However, Mg(OH)2 has low solubility and requires ionization with an HCl solution [41]. Once converted to Mg2+, the carbonation reaction was used to produce MgCO3. Finally, Li2CO3 was produced by a reaction with Na2CO3, which was produced after CO2 capture to separate Li+ from desalination wastewater. To produce high-purity Li2CO3, temperatures of 353–363 K were required, for which recovered 340 K NGCC waste heat was used to reduce energy consumption.

Fig. 2 shows a flow diagram of the lithium production process using waste heat from the proposed NGCC process and seawater desalination wastewater. In this study, the process model was simulated using Aspen Plus V11.0. The following assumptions were made:
•
The process is operated under steady-state conditions.
•
The flue-gas components discharged from the NGCC process consisted of N2 (1 wt%), CH4 (97 wt%), C2H6 (1 wt%), and C3H8 (1 wt%) [42].
•
The seawater temperature and pressure were set at 293 K and 1 bar, respectively [43].
•
The high-pressure and low-pressure turbines were modeled as a single turbine.
•
The process of reusing the condensed steam after passing through the NGCC capacitor in the HRSG was simulated as an open loop.

The process proposed in this study was simulated using Aspen Plus software with the ELECNRTL thermodynamic model. The ELECNRTL equations used in the simulation were as follows Eqs. (1) and (2) [44]:

The recovery of metal cations in ionic form from desalination wastewater was facilitated using the ELECNRTL thermodynamic model to simulate and achieve chemical equilibrium in the aqueous phase.

In this study, to condense the BOG gas that regasified because of the temperature difference inside the LNG tank before proceeding with the LNG regasification reaction, a compressor (COMP1) was used to increase the pressure to 3 bar, thereby reducing the energy consumption. The condensed BOG was further condensed with the liquid using a condenser (COND). Next, the condensed BOG was pumped together with the LNG from 1 bar to 60 bar using a pump (PUMP1) and then regasified by applying heat above the boiling point of the LNG through a regasification reactor (VAPOR). The LNG regasification reaction increased the temperature of the 117 K LNG to 293 K. Seawater was used as the heat source for the regasification reaction, and a considerable amount of 279 K LNG cold energy was wasted. In this process, waste LNG cold energy was recovered and utilized to lower the temperature of the K+ separation process (HX-1) and capture process (HX-2) of flue gas generated in the NGCC process. Table 1 lists the BOG and LNG feed parameters used in this study. The condensers and vaporizers were modeled using a heater and a HeatX reactor, respectively.

In our process, the natural gas generated by LNG regasification reacted with the air in the combustor (COMBUST) and was burned. During this process, a flue gas with a temperature of approximately 2,493 K was produced, which was then used to generate a large amount of electricity through a gas turbine (GT). The flue gas, which still contained a significant amount of energy after passing through the gas turbine, was then passed through HRSG to produce a high-temperature and high-pressure steam, used to generate additional electricity. The high-energy steam produced in the HRSG was used to generate electricity using a steam turbine (ST). To maintain the steam energy, a condenser (COND2) was used to recover the steam energy after power generation at low pressure. Seawater was used as the heat source to condense the steam. However, the recondensation of steam with seawater generates a large amount of thermal energy. Therefore, in this study, waste heat energy was recovered and used to preheat the draw solution during the seawater desalination process (HX-3) and the precipitation reaction of high-purity lithium (HX-4). Finally, the condensed low-pressure steam was reused as feed for the HRSG after the pressure was raised to 75 bar using a pump (PUMP2). Table 2 lists the model design parameters for the LNG regasification and NGCC processes. The combustor was simulated using the RGibbs model, which calculates the equilibrium phase change minimization. The HRSG and condenser for steam condensation were modeled using a HeatX reactor.

The seawater desalination process in this study used a hybrid FO-RO system, and the compositions of the feed stream (FS) and concentrated seawater stream (CONC-FS) are listed in Table 3 . In the FO process, freshwater was produced by the osmotic difference between the feed and draw solutions passing through a semipermeable membrane. The freshwater recovery rate of the FO process was set to 63%, and the recovered water was transferred to the RO process [50]. The remaining concentrated seawater (CONC-FS) containing metal cations was recovered to produce high-added value byproducts. Water recovered through the FO process was pumped to 30 bar using a pump (PUMP) before being transferred to the RO process. Unlike FO, RO uses a pressure gradient to pass through a permeable membrane and separate freshwater and draw solutions. The rejection rate of monovalent NaCl of the permeable membrane during the RO process is 99%, so it was set that 99% of the drawing solution was separated in the RO model [51]. Therefore, depending on the mass balance, the same amount of freshwater was separated in the RO as in the FO process. The draw solution discarded in the RO stage was reused by preheating it to a temperature of 318 K using a heater (HX-3) after reducing the pressure to 1 bar through a valve. The hybrid FO-RO process was simulated using a Sep reactor to simplify the modeling of the desalination process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lithium recovery process using desalination wastewater modeling
ce:section.ce:para: The following steps were modeled to produce lithium using desalination wastewater containing concentrated seawater. First, to separate K+, which is present in desalination wastewater, it should react with HClO4 to produce KClO4. The reaction that produced high-purity KClO4 occurs at 266.15 K. The discarded LNG cold energy from Section 2.2.1, was recovered, cooled from 303 K to 281 K, and used to reduce the required energy. KClO4, produced by the precipitation reaction of K+, was separated using HClO4 according to the reaction formula as following Eq.

KClO4, produced by the precipitation reaction of K+, was separated using HClO4 according to the reaction formula as follows Eq. (4) [36]:

KCLO4 formation was modeled using an RCSTR reactor, and the resulting KClO4 was separated using the Sep model.

The concentrated seawater from which K+ was separated produced NaOH, which could be used as a pH-adjusting solvent and CO2 absorbent through a chlor-alkali electrolysis reaction. The equation for generating NaOH via electrolysis is as follows Eqs. (5)-(8) [53]:

NaOH produced by electrolysis was mixed with concentrated seawater to precipitate Mg2+ as Mg(OH)2 via a pH swing process. Mg2+ reacted with NaOH at pH 7–11 and its precipitate was separated using the Sep model. Ca2+ is precipitated into Ca(OH)2 through an additional pH swing process using NaOH. Ca(OH)2 was precipitated at pH 10–12, and the precipitated Ca(OH)2 was separated using the Sep model. Ca2+ and Mg2+ precipitation were modeled using the RCSTR. The formulas for the precipitation of Mg(OH)2 and Ca(OH)2 during the pH swing process are as follows Eqs. (9) and (10) [54,55]:

The flue gas emitted from NGCC power plants contains significant amounts of CO2. To capture CO2 from flue gas, the NaOH produced by the electrolysis was used as an absorbent. The NaOH absorbent was injected into the upper part of the absorption tower, while the flue gas was injected into the lower end. The CO2 capture reaction takes place through gas–liquid contact inside the absorber using the NaOH absorbent, via the following Eqs. (11)-(15) [56]:

Table 4 lists the design parameters of the model used to simulate the CO2 absorbers. The diameter of the absorber is selected to ensure that the flooding at each stage did not exceed 65% [57]. A CO2 capture model was designed using a rate-based Rradfrac reactor.
The CO2 captured using the NaOH absorbent existed in the ionized form of Na2CO3. This ionic CO2 then underwent a carbonation reaction with Mg(OH)2 and Ca(OH)2, forming MgCO3 and CaCO3.

To react the separated Mg(OH)2 with ionized CO2 to produce MgCO3, the water-insoluble Mg(OH)2 was reacted with HCl to form water-soluble MgCl2, using the following Eq. (16) [59]:

Finally, the Li+ in the concentrated seawater was precipitated with Na2CO3 to form Li2CO3. The reaction required a high temperature of 353–363 K to produce high-purity Li2CO3. The lithium production process feed (FS-LI) temperature is 289 K, which requires high energy to produce high-purity lithium, but the proposed process preheats the lithium production stream by using waste heat of about 340 K from the steam re-condensation process. The stream temperature was then preheated to 338 K (HFS-L), and additional energy was added to bring the temperature to 358 K for high-purity lithium production. Therefore, recovering 340 K of NGCC waste heat saved considerable energy for high-purity lithium production. To model the proposed process, the pH swing and carbonation reactions were simulated using an RCST reactor, and a Sep reactor was used to separate the products produced via the pH swing process. Furthermore, the process of converting Mg(OH)2 to MgCl2 using an HCl solution was simulated using an RStoic reactor with the fraction conversion set to 0.6.

To demonstrate the economic feasibility of the proposed process, it was compared with the base process to validate and calculate the net present value (NPV) and payback period (PBP). To calculate the NPV and PBP, the CAPEX and OPEX of the base and proposed processes were obtained. Table 5 shows the methodological approach for the CAPEX estimation. CAPEX was priced by the FCI based on the cost of the equipment. The FCI was defined as the sum of the direct and indirect costs associated with plant installation costs, including project contingencies.

The equipment costs of the compressor, LNG storage tank, NGCC, electrolysis, and precipitation processes were determined based on the following Eq. (28):

Tables 6 and 7 show the methodological approach for OPEX estimation and operating expenditure. The OPEX was estimated according to the FCI, as shown in Table 6. The utility costs (e.g., electricity and chilled water) and raw material costs used to calculate the OPEX are listed in Table 7. Also, the annual process assumed an uptime of 8,460 h and an annual discount rate of 7.0%. An economic evaluation of the proposed process is presented in Section 3.2.

The simulation results for LNG regasification, NGCC, desalination, and lithium production processes are presented in Section 3.1. Additionally, in Section 3.2 the economic evaluation method is outlined and results of comparing the proposed process with the base case are provided.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation results of LNG regasification and NGCC power plant process
ce:section.ce:para: A condensation reaction was performed to convert the BOG into a liquid fraction with a vapor fraction of 1. The liquid fraction underwent a phase change and became fully condensed. Subsequently, both the LNG and condensed BOG were pumped to 60 bar. While passing through the vaporizer, the LNG was heated by seawater until it reached its boiling point, which was higher than 283 K. As a result, a vapor fraction of 1 NG was generated during the LNG regasification process, which involved regasifying LNG and BOG together to produce NG. Seawater was used as the heating agent for the LNG regasification reaction, resulting in a significant amount of LNG cold energy waste of approximately 2,537 tons/h. However, this LNG cold energy is recovered and used in HX-1 and HX-2 to save energy. Table 8 presents the simulation results for each stream during LNG regasification.

As a result, the LNG regasification process regasified 31.718 kg/s of LNG to produce 31.718 kg/s of NG, i.e., a regasification reaction of 99.9%. The NG generated by the regasification reaction is used to generate electricity through the NGCC process.

The NG regasified by the LNG gasification process reacted with the air inside the combustor, producing a high-temperature exothermic reaction that resulted in the discharge of flue gas at approximately 2,493 K. The CH4, C2H6, and C3H8 that were present in the natural gas underwent an internal reaction in the combustor and reacted with O2 in the air, leading to the emission of 342.4 tons/h of CO2 at a 100% conversion rate. The high-temperature gas generated by the combustor passed through a gas turbine to generate 1,687.79 MW of electricity. Despite passing through the gas turbine, a significant amount of heat energy remained at 1,482 K. Therefore, the flue gas passes through the HRSG to produce high-temperature steam. The steam produced by the HRSG contains a large amount of energy at 975 K and is used to generate electricity through a steam turbine (HPT, LPT), producing 386.57 MW of electricity. Table 9 shows the electricity produced by the NGCC and the flue gas composition.

The remaining steam was condensed and discharged. Although seawater is used as the heat source for condensing steam, the condensation reaction results in the removal of high-temperature waste heat. To address this issue, this research recovers the desalination wastewater discarded at around 340 K and uses it for HX-3 and HX-4. This waste heat recovery method reduces the energy consumption of the process. The condensed steam is then pumped to a pressure of 75 bar and reused during the HRSG process. Table 10 presents the simulation results for each stream in the NGCC process.

31.718 kg/s of NG reacted with 1,263.9 kg/s of air to produce 2,497.2 K and 1,295.7 kg/s of flue gas. About 4.28% CO2 is released from the flue gas generated by the NGCC process, and flue gas treatment is essential. The process simulation results for CO2 capture in this study are shown in Section 3.1.4. As a result, the gas turbine in the NGCC process produces 1,687.79 MW of electricity, and the steam turbine produces 386.57 MW of electricity. The electricity generated through the NGCC process can be sold to generate revenue.

A draw solution with a NaCl concentration of 70,000 ppm was used in the FO process to produce freshwater, and this process generated 6,544.7 tons/h of concentrated seawater as a by-product. The pressure of the freshwater collected from the FO process was increased to approximately 30 bar, and the RO process produced 13,372.9 tons/h of freshwater. As a result, the hybrid FO-RO process recovered approximately 13,372.9 tons/h of freshwater. Discharged desalination wastewater had a high salinity and high concentration of metal cations. Therefore, desalination wastewater was used to simulate the recovery of metal cations. As a result, 4,544.7 tons/h of seawater desalination wastewater can be used to produce lithium in wastewater through the lithium recovery process, and 13,372.9 tons/h of fresh water can be sold to earn revenue. The RO process is typically used to desalinize seawater. However, the RO process has high energy usage and low water recovery rates of less than 50%. However, the hybrid FO-RO process can not only reduce RO membrane fouling but also reduce RO process operation and maintenance costs and increase water recovery [50]. As a result, by increasing the water recovery rate, seawater freshwater wastewater containing high concentrations of lithium and metal cations could be obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation results of lithium recovery process from desalination wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: A process to separate Li+ from seawater desalination wastewater that contains K+, Na+, Mg2+, and Ca2+ ions was performed. First, K+ was separated by precipitating it as KClO4 owing to its solubility difference by reacting it with the HClO4 solution. The results confirmed that 178.1 kmol/h of K+ reacted with 177.9 kmol/h of HClO4, resulting in ∼99.8% conversion. This process was performed at a pH below 2 and a temperature of 266 K, as determined by Chen et al. [36] Fig. 3 shows the precipitation rate of KClO4. Second, NaOH produced through electrolysis was added to adjust the pH of the desalination water from which K+ was separated to 7–11, resulting in the separation of 962.5 kmol/h of Mg2+ into 962.4 kmol/h of Mg(OH)2, with a conversion of ∼99.9%, which confirmed the removal of most of the Mg2+ ions from the desalination water. Third, the pH was adjusted to 11–13 by adding NaOH to dissociate Ca2+ ions. As a result, 78.5 kmol/h of Ca2+ reacted to form 78.5 kmol/h of Ca(OH)2, resulting in a conversion of ∼99.9%. Fig. 4 shows the precipitation rates of Ca(OH)2. The pH swing process produced 177.9 ton/h of KClO4, 962.4 ton/h of Mg(OH)2, and 78.5 ton/h of Ca(OH)2, which reacted with ionic CO2 to produce magnesium carbonate and calcium carbonate through a carbonation process. Table 11 shows the simulation results for each stream in the cation precipitation process from the desalination wastewater.

To capture the flue gas CO2 emitted from the NGCC process, a CO2 capture process was simulated using electrolyzed NaOH as the absorbent. 998.93 kmol/h NaOH absorbent was used at the top of the absorption tower to capture 7,057.62 kmol/h of CO2 entering the bottom of the absorption tower. Table 12 shows the simulation result for CO2 capture efficiency, kinetics, and equilibrium parameters of the carbon capture and utilization (CCU).

Consequently, approximately 93.0% of CO2 was captured, and the captured carbon dioxide was exhausted and utilized in ionized form. The ionic CO2 discharged from the absorber and Mg(OH)2 and Ca(OH)2 separated through the pH swing process were carbonated to produce MgCO3 and CaCO3. First, the separated Mg(OH)2 was converted to MgCl2 using an HCl solution before the carbonation reaction. Mg(OH)2 has a low solubility of 6.4 × 10−4 g/100 mL, while MgCl2 has a high solubility of 54.3 g/100 mL. Therefore, 962.4 kmol/h of Mg(OH)2 was ionized to 960.6 kmol/h of Mg2+ by reaction with 1,923 kmol/h of HCl. Subsequently, Mg2+ and ionized CO2 react with Na2CO3 to produce MgCO3. The carbonation reaction produced 927.6 kmol/h of MgCO3 with a conversion of approximately ∼ 48.2% to MgCO3. Second, the separated Ca(OH)2 reacts with Na2CO3 to produce CaCO3. Unlike Mg(OH)2, Ca(OH)2 is highly soluble and does not require ion dissociation using HCl. The CaCO3 produced by the reaction of Ca(OH)2 with ionized CO2 was 78.5 kmol/h, and approximately 99.9% of CaCO3 was produced by the carbonation reaction. Finally, to produce lithium from concentrated seawater, it reacts with Na2CO3 containing ionized CO2 to form Li2CO3. High-temperature conditions of approximately 353–363 K are required to produce high-purity Li2CO3. Although this process requires a large amount of energy, the proposed process recovers the waste heat from the NGCC, which is discarded at approximately 342 K, resulting in energy savings. As a result, 0.4374 kmol/h of Li+ reacts with Na2CO3 to form 0.217 kmol/h of Li2CO3, resulting in a conversion rate of approximately 97.6%. If the process capacity is scaled up, large amounts of desalination wastewater can be used to recover more Li. Table 13 shows the simulation results for each stream in the carbonation process of desalination wastewater.

The resulting MgCO3 is used in flooring and materials used in construction, such as flooring, rubber admixtures, insulation, etc. CaCO3 makes up the stalactites in limestone pupils and is used as chalk for blackboards and lime for agriculture. Finally, the resulting Li2CO3 is used as a material for lithium balls, a key raw material in lithium batteries, as well as a strong fusing agent in the high melting point range.

In this section, the economic viability of the proposed process is demonstrated by comparing the economic evaluations of the base case and the proposed process. Section 3.2.1 shows the amount of energy saved by recovering LNG cold energy and NGCC waste heat. Section 3.2.2 compares and summarizes the economic evaluation results of the proposed and base processes are compared and summarized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy saving by LNG cold heat and NCGG waste heat recovery.
ce:section.ce:para: In Section 2.1, Fig. 1 shows a schematic of the cold-heat and waste-heat recovery flows of the proposed process. First, the cold energy generated during LNG regasification was recovered and utilized in the K+ precipitation (HX-1) and CO2 capture (HX-2) processes. The energy of 108.1 MW is required to adjust K+ to 266 K for precipitation in KClO4. However, by recovering the LNG cold and heat in this process, 89.2 MW of heat is supplied from HX-1, saving 18.9 MW of energy. Second, LNG cold energy was employed to lower the temperature of the flue gas before capturing the CO2 in the flue gas. The energy required to cool the flue gas at 353 K before LNG cold recovery was 142.1 MW, but the energy required 0 energy after LNG cold recovery, enabling cooling of all the flue gas without any additional energy use. Third, the waste heat generated when condensing steam in the NGCC power plant process is retrieved and used for the draw solution preheating reaction. The waste heat generated in the NGCC process was discharged at 333 K, and a certain amount was recovered and used in the reaction to preheat the draw solution to 318 K. The energy required before waste heat recovery was 10.7 MW, after waste heat recovery, the energy required was reduced to 0 MW, thereby reducing the energy required by HX-3. Finally, the waste heat from the NGCC process is reclaimed and utilized in the process of precipitating Li2CO3. To obtain high-purity Li2CO3, the reaction should be carried out at 353–363 K, which requires 731.4 MW of energy. However, by recovering the NGCC process waste heat, 540.7 MW of energy was saved, and only 190.7 MW of energy was required. Therefore, by recovering LNG cold and NGCC waste heat, a total of 751.92 MW of energy in the proposed process was reduced, resulting in about 48.7% energy savings. Table 14 compares the amount of energy required before and after waste heat recovery.

To demonstrate the validity and economic feasibility of the proposed process, we used the economic evaluation method described in Section 2.3. The base process, including LNG regasification, NGCC, and desalination, was used as a benchmark for comparison. Fig. 5 shows a comparison of the equipment costs between the base and proposed processes.

The cost of the equipment for the lithium recovery process in the proposed process increased by 9.45% compared to the base process, which is equivalent to 32,191,991 USD. Among the additional equipment required for the proposed process, the absorber, which has the highest cost, accounts for 36.6% of the total additional equipment cost, followed by the electrolyte, which accounts for 8.85%. The detailed equipment costs of the proposed and base processes are listed in Table 15 .

To evaluate the NPV and PBP economics, the CAPEX and FCI for the proposed and base processes are listed in Table 16 . The total equipment cost required for the process proposed in this study was 339,014,536 USD, and the FCI was 1,130,048,453 USD. In comparison, the total equipment cost requested by the base process was 306,812,271 USD with an FCI of 1,022,707,569 USD. Thus, the proposed process increased the FCI by 107,340,884 USD compared with the base process.

The CAPEX of the base process was 1,431,790,597 USD, and the CAPEX of the proposed process with the lithium recovery process added to the base process was 1,582,067,834 USD. Compared with the base process, the CAPEX of the proposed process increased by 150,277,237 USD due to the increased cost of equipment to recover lithium and metal cations in concentrated wastewater and to capture CO2 emitted from the NGCC process.

Fig. 6 shows the OPEX results and ratios of the base and proposed processes. The highest percentage of the base process OPEX was raw material cost, which accounted for 38.5% of the total OPEX. Next, maintenance and labor materials accounted for 21.9%, and insurance and tax accounted for the lowest percentage at 3.03%. In contrast, the highest rate of the proposed process OPEX was utility cost, which accounted for 26.2%. This was followed by raw materials, maintenance, and labor materials, with the second highest percentage at 21.8%. Finally, insurance and taxes accounted for the lowest rate at 1.36%.

Table 17 lists the operating expenditures of the base and proposed processes. The base process had a lower OPEX value of 338,067,374 USD compared to the proposed process because it did not go through the process of separating ions in the concentrated wastewater, such as Li+, K+, Mg2+, and Ca2+ or capturing the CO2 emitted from the NGCC process. In contrast, the OPEX of the proposed process is 831,074,100 USD, which is 2.46 times more expensive than that of the base process.

To understand the benefits gained by installing an additional ion recovery unit compared to the base process, the revenues of the base and proposed processes were examined. Table 18 shows the annual revenue of the base processes, which include electricity and freshwater production through the NGCC and desalination processes. Gas and steam turbines generate 2,074 MW of electricity, resulting in a revenue of 1,362,861.5 USD/y. Additionally, seawater desalination produces 82,059,000 tons/y of freshwater, resulting in a revenue of 1,439,506,978 USD/y. Thus, the total annual revenue from the base process is 4,311,205.5 USD/y.

Table 19 lists the annual revenue of the proposed process. In addition to the electricity and freshwater produced by the base processes, the proposed process generates valuable products by recovering lithium-containing metal cations from seawater desalination wastewater. The proposed process produced high-value products, such as KClO4, MgCO3, and CaCO3, generating a total revenue of 2,383,287,790 USD/y. Furthermore, the recovery of 108.5 tons/y of lithium carbonate from seawater desalination wastewater, which is a high-value product, generated additional revenue of 1,301,904 USD/y. Therefore, the total revenue generated by the proposed process was 3,824,096,672 USD/y.

Table 20 shows the operating capital costs required for the proposed process compared to the base process. The proposed process required additional 92,755 tons/y of HClO4 and 319,793 tons/y of HCl for K+ separation and Mg2+ dissociation, respectively. Consequently, 130,122,200 USD/y of capital costs is required.

A carbon tax is imposed on the amount of carbon emitted into the atmosphere to minimize carbon emissions. In the proposed process, 210,862 tons/y CO2 were emitted as a result of the NGCC process, which was 7.0% of the total emissions. Therefore, a carbon tax of 8,434,480 USD/y should be charged. However, the baseline process does not capture any CO2; therefore, a large carbon tax should be charged. The CO2 emitted from the NGCC process in the base process was 3,001,330 tons/y, and the carbon tax was set at 40 USD/ton, which required charging 120,053,200 USD/y. The NPV was calculated using the CAPEX, OPEX, revenue, and carbon tax for the base and proposed processes. The NPV of the proposed process are listed in Table 21 . The CAPEX and OPEX of the proposed process were higher than those of the base process, but the annual revenue was 2.66 times higher than that of the base process. This is due to two factors. First, the proposed process generates a high value-added by-product by capturing metal cations in discarded wastewater. Second, not only is carbon dioxide captured and no carbon tax imposed, but ionized carbon dioxide is also used in the carbonation process. Finally, it reduces energy requirements by utilizing the waste heat emitted from LNG regasification and NGCC processes. Owing to these factors, the NPV of the proposed process was 3.37 times higher than that of the base process.

The PBP was obtained from the cash flow diagram by observing the point at which cash flow increased to the location of negative working capital. The PBP determines how long it takes to recover the original amount invested in a project, excluding the land and working capital costs. To calculate the PBP of the base process and the proposed process, the equation presented in Section 2.3 was used to calculate the PBP and found that the PBP of the base process is 1.29 y and the PBP of the proposed process is 0.31 y, indicating that the PBP of the proposed process is shorter.

Table 21 shows the prominent techno-economic analysis results, such as the CAPEX, OPEX, Revenue, carbon tax, NPV, and PBP, for the base and proposed processes. The CAPEX and OPEX of the proposed process were higher than those of the base process; however, the revenue was 2.66 times higher than that of the existing process. Moreover, the NPV is 3.37 times higher than that of the base process, and the PBP is 0.98 y shorter. Therefore, the economic efficiency of the proposed process is higher than that of the base process.

This work presents a novel process for lithium production using the waste heat from NGCC and desalination wastewater as a source, making a significant contribution to the existing literature. First, the integration of the hybrid desalination process with the lithium production process can increase the lithium production rate without additional pollutant discharge. Second, because Na2CO3 can be recovered from desalination wastewater during the CO2 capture process, the environmental issues caused by desalination wastewater discharge and CO2 in the flue gas can be solved simultaneously, while also reducing the raw material cost for lithium production. Moreover, because NGCC waste heat can be utilized to precipitate Li2CO3, the operating costs can be reduced by utilizing high-temperature conditions, thus increasing the economic feasibility. The results of this study indicate that 108.5 tons/y of Li2CO3 can be produced using concentrated wastewater generated by the FO-PO hybrid desalination process, capturing approximately 93.0% of CO2 and achieving 48.7% energy savings by recovering waste heat. Additionally, the net present value and payback period of the proposed process were determined to be 2,827 million USD and 0.31 years, which is 3.37 times higher and 0.98 years lower than the base process, respectively. Therefore, the proposed system can achieve efficient lithium production using waste heat in a cost-effective and environmentally friendly manner. However, this study proposed a conceptual design process that combines desalination wastewater and NGCC waste heat to produce energy-efficient lithium. Thus, an experimental study is needed to confirm that the two processes can be well combined to produce high-purity lithium when the proposed process is applied to an actual plant.

Hyejeong Lee: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jonghun Lim: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Junghwan Kim: Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.
